text: The patient is a seventy-eight year old female with a past medical history of stage three colon cancer diagnosed in two thousand twelve. She is status post exploratory laparotomy and sigmoid resection followed by adjuvant chemotherapy.
Postoperatively, she developed a ventral incisional hernia.
The surgeon initiates dissection by developing the left retro-rectus space, moving inferiorly past the arcuate line towards Cooper's ligament.
The surgeon achieves safe port placement under direct vision after developing the left retro-rectus space.
The surgeon develops the left retrorectus space medially towards the hernia defect at the linea alba using monopolar cautery, sharp dissection, and blunt dissection techniques.
The surgeon uses sharp dissection to incise the linea alba below its decussations at the midline, creating an opening in the central abdominal wall.

In the mid-abdomen, the surgeon continues the incision of the peritoneum inferiorly, revealing the Swiss cheese hernia defect. The hernia contents are visualized and reduced back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon continues dissection inferiorly at the arcuate line within the preperitoneal space, further expanding the surgical field.
The surgeon drops the preperitoneal flap from the midline, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues dissection cranially towards the falciform ligament and retroxiphoid fat pad, entering the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon incises the contralateral rectus sheath and dissects it to expose the retrorectus space, similar to previous dissection techniques.
The surgeon connects the preperitoneal flap to the contralateral flap inferiorly, completing the flap formation in the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon continues dissection of the right retro-rectus space towards the linea semilunaris, taking care to preserve the neurovascular bundles visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon evaluates the posterior flaps, inspecting their positioning and integrity within the surgical field.
The surgeon measures the hernia defect, noting the largest supraumbilical defect is four by four centimeters, and the total defect length is twelve centimeters.
The surgeon closes the midline defect with a running O permanent suture, visibly approximating the tissue edges along the defect line.
The surgeon closes the posterior flap using a 2-0 semi-permanent suture in a running modified canal stitch fashion, securing the flap in place.

The surgeon selects a mid-weight polypropylene mesh, trims it to a size of fourteen by twenty-five centimeters, and introduces it into the retro-rectus space. The mesh is then unrolled and laid flat within the surgical field.
The surgeon applies 20 milliliters of fibrin sealant over the mesh in the retro-rectus space to achieve fixation and hemostasis.
The patient was discharged home post-op day number four with minimal narcotic use and her short-term follow-up was without major post-operative complications.
